steps.title: Identify the core tension in the passage
steps.content: The strongest clue is the logical structure: consumers demand increasingly fast delivery, companies innovate to meet those demands, but every innovation “engenders its own complications.” This pattern suggests that attempting to fulfill consumer expectations creates unavoidable problems—a sign that the expectations themselves may be fundamentally unrealistic.

steps.title: Match the logical conclusion to the choices
steps.content:
Choice D (‘consumers' expectations for reduced delivery times may be outstripping what is viable for delivery companies to provide.’) states that consumer expectations “may be outstripping what is viable for delivery companies to provide.” Outstripping means exceeding what is actually achievable.

If every innovation to meet delivery speed fails because it introduces new complications, then the underlying problem is not poor execution or lack of investment—it's that the expectation itself may exceed what's realistically possible.

The other choices assume more resources and better planning will help, which contradicts the passage's point that innovation itself backfires; choice C (‘there may not be sufficient incentive for delivery companies to attempt to solve the problems associated with last-mile logistics.’) suggests insufficient incentive, but the passage shows companies are innovating, just unsuccessfully.

That confirms D is correct.
